title: Dr Reddy's gets nod to roll out generic oral semaglutide drug
summary: The formulation was clinically evaluated in a head-to-head comparative study conducted in India, which established the non-inferiority to Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide (Rybelsus brand). Based on this clinical evidence, our product has received regulatory...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Synopsis

"The formulation was clinically evaluated in a head-to-head comparative study conducted in India, which established the non-inferiority to Novo Nordisk's oral semaglutide (Rybelsus brand). Based on this clinical evidence, our product has received regulatory approval in India under the biosimilar regulatory pathway," a company spokesperson said.

Listen to this article in summarized format

MUMBAI: Dr Reddy’s Labs has received the final approval from India’s drug regulator to manufacture and sell generic versions of semaglutide tablets, a popular weight-loss and anti-diabetes drug, even as innovator Novo Nordisk battles Indian drugmakers over alleged patent infringement.

While the core semaglutide patent expired in India last month, Novo’s patent on oral formulations runs until 2031. The Danish drugmaker sells the tablet under the brand name Rybelsus.

Hyderabad-based Dr Reddy’s confirmed the Central Drug Standard Control Organisation’s (CDSCO) approval on Wednesday, but did not share a launch timeline.

Also Read: Iran war impact: Essential drugs may cost up to 5% more, for now

“The formulation was clinically evaluated in a head-to-head comparative study conducted in India, which established the non-inferiority to Novo Nordisk’s oral semaglutide (Rybelsus brand). Based on this clinical evidence, our product has received regulatory approval in India under the biosimilar regulatory pathway,” a company spokesperson said.

Earlier, a committee of experts gave a positive opinion on the company’s application based on data obtained from local clinical trials.

The development sets the stage for more intense competition in the country’s weight-loss and anti-diabetes drug market that has zoomed past Rs 1,600-crore mark in just over a year.

Novo has moved the Delhi High Court, alleging patent infringement of semaglutide oral formulations by Dr Reddy’s and Torrent Pharma.

Also Read: Drug regulator halves approval timelines, eases several rules

The two Indian companies have argued that SNAC (salcaprozate sodium) levels in the drugs developed separately by their research teams are different from the innovator’s drugs. SNAC technology enables absorption in drug compositions.

Medical experts said the Indian companies used additional enhancers and therefore their products don’t infringe Novo’s patent. The claimed molar range for Novo’s drugs is 0.65-2.1 mmol per tablet.

“We have independently developed our oral semaglutide formulation by incorporating SNAC within a defined and optimized concentration range,” Dr Reddy’s said.

It has won approval for three oral compositions of 3 mg, 7 mg and 14 mg, according to the regulator’s note seen by ET. It is indicated as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycaemic control in adults with type-2 diabetes mellitus.

The manufacturing site of Dr Reddy’s drug substance (raw material) is Hangzhou Zhongemeihuadong Pharmaceutical Jiangdong Co, while the product (tablets) will be made at a facility in Andhra Pradesh.

Ahmedabad-based Torrent Pharma was the first homegrown drugmaker to be granted approval for oral semaglutide about three months ago. Torrent is the only company to have launched both oral and injectable semaglutide.

A spokesperson for Torrent Pharma said the starting dose for its oral drug brands Sembolic and Semalix is launched at half of the price of the innovator drug.

Novo’s Rybelsus costs Rs 10,000–13,000 per month. The once-a-day diabetes and weight-loss medication was launched in India in 2022.

Oral semaglutide market in India currently stands at around Rs 500-600 crore.

On the court case, Torrent noted the company was to not sell the drug in India till expiry of the semaglutide patent on March 20.

Sun Pharma is also actively pursuing a regulatory clearance for oral semaglutide.

While the market for semaglutide in injectable and pen-device forms is expected to see a fierce fight over the next six months, the orals segment may see limited competition primarily because the cost of development of oral semaglutide is significantly higher than the cartridges or vials.

“Oral bioavailability (the amount of the drug that reaches the bloodstream when taken by mouth) of semaglutide is only 7-10%. And hence to make those higher dosages you need 10 times more API than an injectable, thus increasing the cost of making the drug,” diabetologist Rajiv Kovil said.
